assessment.title: Data Science Engineer Onboarding Assessment
assessment.description: An adaptive placement assessment for Data Science Engineers. This bank contains 100 questions (10 per skill). During onboarding, 2 questions per skill are randomly selected to produce a unique 20-question assessment for each student. This is not a certification exam.

selection.note: Randomly draw 2 questions from each skill's 10-question pool at onboarding time to build a unique assessment per student.

assessment.instructions: Select one option for each question. The correct answer and its explanation are revealed only after you click an option. Answer honestly; this test places you on the roadmap and is not graded as a pass/fail exam.

questions.question: Which Python type stores an ordered, mutable collection?
questions.explanation: A list is an ordered, changeable collection.
questions.roadmap_reference: Python for Data Science > Data Structures

questions.question: How do you store key-value pairs in Python?
questions.explanation: A dict maps keys to values.

questions.question: Which loop iterates over each item in a list?
questions.explanation: A for loop iterates over each element.

questions.question: How do you use pandas in a script?
questions.explanation: import pandas as pd is the standard import.

questions.question: Which keyword defines a function?
questions.explanation: def defines a function in Python.

questions.question: What does lst[0:3] return?
questions.explanation: Slicing lst[0:3] returns elements at indices 0,1,2.

questions.question: Which tool installs data science packages like scikit-learn?
questions.explanation: pip installs Python packages.

questions.question: What does [x**2 for x in range(3)] produce?
questions.explanation: It squares 0,1,2 giving [0, 1, 4].

questions.question: What is a lambda in Python?
questions.explanation: A lambda is a small anonymous function, e.g. lambda x: x+1.

questions.question: Which construct handles a possible runtime error?
questions.explanation: try/except catches exceptions gracefully.

questions.question: The 'mean' of a dataset is its...
questions.explanation: The mean is the arithmetic average.

questions.question: The median is...
options: The middle value when data is sorted
questions.explanation: The median is the middle value of sorted data.

questions.question: The mode is...
questions.explanation: The mode is the value that appears most often.

questions.question: Standard deviation measures...
options: How spread out the data is
questions.explanation: Standard deviation measures dispersion around the mean.

questions.question: The range of a dataset is...
questions.explanation: Range is the difference between the maximum and minimum.

questions.question: A probability value must be between...
questions.explanation: Probabilities range from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain).

questions.question: An outlier is a value that...
questions.explanation: An outlier lies far from the bulk of the data.

questions.question: A correlation of -0.9 between two variables indicates...
questions.explanation: Values near -1 indicate a strong inverse relationship (not causation).

questions.question: In a normal distribution, roughly 68% of values fall within...
questions.explanation: About 68% lie within +/-1 standard deviation (the 68-95-99.7 rule).

questions.question: A small p-value (e.g. < 0.05) typically suggests...
questions.explanation: A small p-value suggests the observed effect is unlikely by chance alone.

questions.question: What is the core NumPy data structure?
questions.explanation: NumPy's core type is the ndarray.

questions.question: What is the standard NumPy import?
questions.explanation: import numpy as np is conventional.

questions.question: Which creates a NumPy array from a list?
questions.explanation: np.array() builds an ndarray from a sequence.

questions.question: A key NumPy advantage over plain lists is...
questions.explanation: NumPy performs fast element-wise (vectorized) operations.

questions.question: What does np.zeros(3) create?
questions.explanation: np.zeros(3) yields [0., 0., 0.].

questions.question: What does array.shape tell you?
questions.explanation: shape gives the size along each dimension.

questions.question: For arrays a and b, a + b performs...
questions.explanation: NumPy adds arrays element by element.

questions.question: 'Broadcasting' in NumPy lets you...
options: Operate on arrays of different but compatible shapes
questions.explanation: Broadcasting stretches shapes so element-wise ops work without copies.

questions.question: Why does a NumPy array have a single dtype?
options: Uniform types enable efficient, fast computation
questions.explanation: A single dtype allows contiguous, efficient numeric operations.

questions.question: np.sum(arr, axis=0) on a 2D array sums...
questions.explanation: axis=0 collapses rows, summing down each column.

questions.question: What is a pandas DataFrame?
options: A 2D labeled table of rows and columns
questions.explanation: A DataFrame is a 2D labeled tabular structure.

questions.question: A pandas Series is...
questions.explanation: A Series is a one-dimensional labeled array.

questions.question: Which function loads a CSV into a DataFrame?
questions.explanation: pd.read_csv() reads a CSV file into a DataFrame.

questions.question: What does df.head() show?
questions.explanation: df.head() returns the first rows (default 5).

questions.question: How do you select the 'age' column?
questions.explanation: df['age'] selects that column as a Series.

questions.question: df.shape returns...
questions.explanation: shape gives the (rows, columns) tuple.

questions.question: df.describe() provides...
questions.explanation: describe() gives count, mean, std, min, quartiles, max.

questions.question: df.groupby('category')['sales'].sum() computes...
questions.explanation: groupby aggregates sales summed within each category.

questions.question: Which detects missing values in a DataFrame?
questions.explanation: df.isnull() flags NaN/missing entries.

questions.question: pd.merge(a, b, on='id') does what?
options: Joins two DataFrames on the id column
questions.explanation: merge joins DataFrames on a key, like a SQL join.

questions.question: The main purpose of data visualization is to...
questions.explanation: Visualization reveals patterns and communicates insight.

questions.question: Which is a core Python plotting library?
questions.explanation: Matplotlib is the foundational plotting library.

questions.question: A bar chart is best for...
questions.explanation: Bar charts compare categorical quantities.

questions.question: A line chart is best for showing...
questions.explanation: Line charts show change/trends over a continuous axis like time.

questions.question: A histogram shows...
options: The distribution of a single numeric variable
questions.explanation: A histogram shows how a numeric variable is distributed.

questions.question: A scatter plot is best for...
options: Showing the relationship between two numeric variables
questions.explanation: Scatter plots reveal relationships/correlation between two variables.

questions.question: Why label chart axes and add a title?
options: So readers understand what is shown
questions.explanation: Clear labels/titles make a chart interpretable.

questions.question: Why are pie charts often discouraged for many categories?
options: Angles are hard to compare precisely
questions.explanation: People compare lengths better than angles, so bars often beat pies.

questions.question: A box plot is especially useful for showing...
questions.explanation: Box plots summarize spread, quartiles, and outliers.

questions.question: A correlation heatmap uses color to show...
questions.explanation: A heatmap encodes correlation magnitudes as colors.

questions.question: Which statement reads rows from a table?
questions.explanation: SELECT retrieves rows.

questions.question: Which clause filters rows?
questions.explanation: WHERE filters by condition.

questions.question: SELECT DISTINCT returns...
questions.explanation: DISTINCT removes duplicate rows from results.

questions.question: Which function counts rows?
questions.explanation: COUNT() counts rows.

questions.question: Which clause sorts results?
questions.explanation: ORDER BY sorts the result set.

questions.question: Which clause returns only the first N rows?
questions.explanation: LIMIT restricts the number of returned rows.

questions.question: Which aggregate returns the mean of a column?
questions.explanation: AVG() computes the average of numeric values.

questions.question: An INNER JOIN returns...
questions.explanation: INNER JOIN keeps rows with matching keys in both tables.

questions.question: GROUP BY with COUNT(*) gives...
questions.explanation: GROUP BY buckets rows so aggregates compute per group.

questions.question: How do you filter groups after aggregation?
questions.explanation: HAVING filters aggregated groups (WHERE filters rows before grouping).

questions.question: Machine learning is about...
options: Learning patterns from data to make predictions
questions.explanation: ML learns patterns from data rather than being explicitly programmed.

questions.question: Supervised learning uses...
questions.explanation: Supervised learning trains on labeled input-output pairs.

questions.question: Clustering is an example of...
questions.explanation: Clustering finds structure in unlabeled data (unsupervised).

questions.question: Predicting a category (spam/not spam) is...
questions.explanation: Classification predicts discrete categories.

questions.question: Predicting a continuous number (like price) is...
questions.explanation: Regression predicts continuous numeric values.

questions.question: In ML, 'features' are...
options: Input variables used to make predictions
questions.explanation: Features are the input variables fed to a model.

questions.question: 'Training' a model means...
options: Fitting it to data to learn patterns
questions.explanation: Training fits model parameters to the data.

questions.question: Overfitting means a model...
options: Fits training data well but generalizes poorly
questions.explanation: Overfitting memorizes training data and fails on unseen data.

questions.question: Why split data into train and test sets?
questions.explanation: A held-out test set estimates generalization to new data.

questions.question: What does k-fold cross-validation do?
options: Rotates train/validation folds for a robust estimate
questions.explanation: k-fold rotates the validation fold, averaging results for a stable estimate.

questions.question: A neural network is made of...
questions.explanation: Neural nets are layers of connected neurons.

questions.question: 'Deep' learning refers to networks with...
questions.explanation: Depth refers to multiple hidden layers.

questions.question: During training, a neural network adjusts its...
questions.explanation: Training updates the connection weights to reduce error.

questions.question: An activation function (like ReLU) adds...
questions.explanation: Activations introduce non-linearity so nets learn complex patterns.

questions.question: Which is a deep learning framework?
questions.explanation: PyTorch (and TensorFlow) are deep learning frameworks.

questions.question: Deep learning excels at tasks like...
questions.explanation: DL powers vision, speech, and language tasks.

questions.question: Why are GPUs used to train deep networks?
options: They accelerate the heavy matrix math in parallel
questions.explanation: GPUs parallelize the matrix operations, speeding training.

questions.question: What does backpropagation do?
questions.explanation: Backpropagation computes gradients used by the optimizer to update weights.

questions.question: One 'epoch' means...
options: One full pass over the training data
questions.explanation: An epoch is a complete pass through the training dataset.

questions.question: The learning rate controls...
options: How big each weight-update step is
questions.explanation: The learning rate scales the step size of weight updates.

questions.question: Feature engineering is...
options: Creating/transforming inputs to help models learn
questions.explanation: It transforms raw data into informative features for models.

questions.question: A common way to handle missing numeric values is...
questions.explanation: Imputation fills missing values (e.g. with mean/median).

questions.question: Why one-hot encode a categorical column?
options: Convert categories into numeric columns models can use
questions.explanation: One-hot encoding turns categories into binary numeric features.

questions.question: Why scale/normalize numeric features?
options: Put them on comparable ranges for many algorithms
questions.explanation: Scaling helps algorithms sensitive to feature magnitude converge well.

questions.question: From a timestamp you might engineer features like...
questions.explanation: Extracting parts like day/hour/month often adds signal.

questions.question: Domain knowledge helps feature engineering by...
questions.explanation: Understanding the domain guides which features matter.

questions.question: 'Binning' a numeric feature means...
questions.explanation: Binning converts a continuous variable into discrete ranges.

questions.question: 'Data leakage' in feature engineering is when...
options: Features use information unavailable at prediction time
questions.explanation: Leakage uses future/target info that won't exist at inference, inflating scores.

questions.question: When scaling, you should fit the scaler on...
options: The training set only, then apply to test
questions.explanation: Fitting on train-only prevents test information from leaking in.

questions.question: Why perform feature selection?
options: Remove irrelevant/redundant features to improve models
questions.explanation: Selecting useful features can improve accuracy and reduce overfitting.

questions.question: Accuracy measures...
questions.explanation: Accuracy is correct predictions divided by total predictions.

questions.question: You evaluate a model on...
options: Held-out test data it did not train on
questions.explanation: Evaluation uses unseen test data to estimate real performance.

questions.question: Which is a classification metric?
questions.explanation: Precision is a classification metric (MSE/R2 are for regression).

questions.question: Which metric is used for regression?
questions.explanation: MSE measures average squared error for regression.

questions.question: A confusion matrix shows...
options: Counts of correct and incorrect predictions per class
questions.explanation: It tabulates true/false positives and negatives per class.

questions.question: High training accuracy but low test accuracy suggests...
questions.explanation: That gap is a classic sign of overfitting.

questions.question: Why compare a model to a simple baseline?
options: To confirm it actually adds value
questions.explanation: A baseline shows whether the model beats a trivial approach.

questions.question: Recall measures the fraction of...
questions.explanation: Recall = true positives / all actual positives.

questions.question: The F1 score is...
options: The harmonic mean of precision and recall
The average of MSE and R2
questions.explanation: F1 balances precision and recall via their harmonic mean.

questions.question: Why can accuracy mislead on imbalanced data?
options: Predicting the majority class can look accurate but be useless
questions.explanation: With rare positives, always predicting the majority yields high accuracy but poor utility.
